coredata.dc:title: A new N/Fe doped carbon dot nanosurface molecularly imprinted polymethacrylate nanoprobe for trace fipronil with SERS/RRS dimode technique
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yue
Qin, Zhiyu
Liang, Aihui
Wen, Guiqing
Jiang, Zhiliang
coredata.dc:description: The N and Fe doped carbon dot (CDNFe) was prepared by microwave procedure. Using CDNFe as the nano-substrate, fipronil (FL) as the template molecule and α-methacrylic acid as the functional monomer, the molecular imprinted polymethacrylic acid nanoprobe (CDNFe@MIP) with difunction was synthesized by microwave procedure. The CDNFe@MIP was characterized by transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier infrared spectroscopy, and other techniques. The results show that the nanoprobe not only distinguish FL but also has a strong catalytic effect on the HAuCl4–Na2C2O4 nanogold indicator reaction. When the nanoprobes specifically recognize FL, their catalytic effect is significantly reduced. Since the AuNPs generated by HAuCl4 reduction have strong surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) and resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) effects, a SERS/RRS dual-mode sensing platform for detecting 5–500 ng/L FL was constructed. The new analytical method was applied to detect FL in food samples with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.3–8.1 % and a recovery rate of 94.6–104.5 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N/Fe-doped carbon dots
Nanosurface molecularly imprinted polymer probes
Nanogold indicator reaction
SRRS/RRS dual-mode sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: On-site generated nanogold SERS/RRS dimode determination of FL based on CDNFe@MIP catalyzed the sodium oxalate reduction of chloroauric acid.

TEM, EDS and XPS. A: TEM of CDNFe (inset: photographs of CDNFe under visible light and 365 nm UV lamp); B: TEM of MIP; C: TEM of CDNFe@MIP; D: TEM of FL- CDNFe@MIP-HAuCl4-Na2C2O4 – HCl; E: EDS of D; F: XPS full spectrum of MIP/CDNFe@MIP; G–H: High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s and O1s peaks of CDNFe@MIP.
Comparison of specific surface area and pore size distribution of MIP, CDNFeMIP and CDNFeMIPFL. A: N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm; B: pore size distribution of MIP, CDNFeMIP and CDNFeMIPFL.

RRS spectra of MIP analysis systems. A: a–g: (0, 20, 50, 150, 300, 400, 500) ng/L FL+6 mg/L MIP+0.05 g/L HAuCl4+0.1875 mmol/L Na2C2O4+0.5 mmol/L HCl; B: a–g: (0, 10, 50, 150, 300, 400, 500) ng/L FL+1.25 mg/L CD@MIP+0.05 g/L HAuCl4+0.1875 mmol/L Na2C2O4+0.5 mmol/L HCl; C: a–g: (0, 10, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500) ng/L FL+1 mg/L CDN@MIP+0.05 g/L HAuCl4+0.1875 mmol/L Na2C2O4+0.5 mmol/L HCl; D: a–g: (0, 5, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500) ng/L FL+0.85 mg/L CDNFe@MIP+0.05 g/LHAuCl4+0.1875 mmol/L Na2C2O4+0.5 mmol/L HCl; E: a–g: (0, 5, 20, 200, 300, 400, 500) ng/L FL+1 mg/L CDNFe@MIP+0.05 g/L HAuCl4+0.1875 mmol/L Na2C2O4+0.5 mmol/L HCl.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The N and Fe doped carbon dot (CDNFe) was prepared by microwave procedure. Using CDNFe as the nano-substrate, fipronil (FL) as the template molecule and α-methacrylic acid as the functional monomer, the molecular imprinted polymethacrylic acid nanoprobe (CDNFe@MIP) with difunction was synthesized by microwave procedure. The CDNFe@MIP was characterized by transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier infrared spectroscopy, and other techniques. The results show that the nanoprobe not only distinguish FL but also has a strong catalytic effect on the HAuCl4–Na2C2O4 nanogold indicator reaction. When the nanoprobes specifically recognize FL, their catalytic effect is significantly reduced. Since the AuNPs generated by HAuCl4 reduction have strong surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) and resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) effects, a SERS/RRS dual-mode sensing platform for detecting 5–500 ng/L FL was constructed. The new analytical method was applied to detect FL in food samples with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.3–8.1 % and a recovery rate of 94.6–104.5 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecular imprinting polymer (MIP) is a technique that customizes binding sites on a carrier through a blot process that produces memory effects on the shape, size, and functional groups of template molecules [1]. In recent years, molecularly imprinted polymers have been widely developed to identify specific target molecules with advantages such as high affinity and selectivity, excellent robustness, chemical stability, long service life, simple synthesis methods, recyclability, and applicability in sensor applications [2]. Although MIP has many advantages, it also has some disadvantages, such as fewer surface imprinting holes and longer equilibrium time. Therefore, in order to improve its performance, researchers have used a variety of nanomaterials such as Au, Pt, Ni nanoparticles and Pt/Au, Au/Ag bimetallic nanoparticles as nano-substrate in electrochemical analysis [2–6]. It can be said that the nanomaterials improve the sensitivity. Carbon dots (CD) refers to carbon-based nanomaterials with unique physicochemical properties, such as ultra-small size, optical properties, chemical stability, biocompatibility, easy synthesis and functionalization, and tunable fluorescence and quantum yield [7–14]. In order to provide CD with unique properties, such as high catalytic ability or recognition of functional groups, heteroatom doping can be carried out by appropriate methods and precursors to synthesize doped carbon dots (CDs). Li et al. [15] used the synthesized calcium-doped carbon dots to show a strong catalytic effect on the reaction of SERS-active gold nanoparticles, and established a SERS sensor for detecting ultra-trace thrombin by combining with aptamers. However, the reported research on the combination of carbon dots and MIP only utilizes the fluorescence characteristics of carbon dots and the recognition function of MIP. The experimental results were greatly affected by the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots, and the sensitivity was low. Sun et al. [16], Ke et al. [17] and Xie et al. [18] embedded CDs as seeds into molecularly imprinted polymers. Combining molecular imprinting technology with carbon dots-based fluorescence detection technology, a highly selective CDs-MIP fluorescent probe was obtained for the detection of pollutants, and the detection limits were 6.7 mg/L, 0.15 nM, and 0.70 μg/L, respectively. To our best knowledge, there is no report on the dual-mode analysis platform of (SERS) and resonance Rayleigh scattering (RRS) for the detection of fipronil (FL) by CDNFe@MIP with catalytic and recognition di-functions.

SERS is an inelastic light scattering analysis method, a powerful spectroscopic technique that utilizes surface-sensitive resonance extensions of standard Raman spectroscopy, which can achieve trace detection of analytes of interest, with significant advantages such as simple operation, fast response, low background, good stability, non-destructive characterization, less sample preparation, no water interference, no labels, and excellent multiplexing ability [19–22]。RRS is an elastic light scattering analysis method, which has developed rapidly in the field of nanocatalytic scale-up analysis due to its simple operation, high cost performance, good reproducibility, fast and sensitive and high sensitivity [23–25]。Zhi et al. [26] synthesized MXene nanosheets by microwave method, and combined the nano-catalytic indicator reaction with the aptamer to construct a SERS/RRS dual-mode biosensor that could detect 4.5 × 10−5 nmol/L isocarbophos. Bai et al. [27] prepared MXene-supported palladium nanoparticles (Ti3C2@Pd) nanosols by a two-step method, and combined the nanocatalytic indicator reaction with specific TNT peptides to establish a simple selective SERS/RRS/Abs three-mode peptide biosensor platform, which was able to detect 1.1–66 pmol/L of TNT with a minimum detection limit of 0.69 pmol/L. Yao et al. [28] used the catalytic amplification signal of polystyrene nanospheres (PN) to conveniently prepare the RRS/SERS dual-mode method to sensitively and selectively detect urea in food. The dual-spectral analysis method combining SERS and RRS can not only verify and complement each other, but also provide two new sensitive detection methods. Therefore, it is necessary to establish a SERS/RRS dual-mode to detect FL.

FL is a phenylpyrazole insecticide used in a variety of agricultural, horticultural and veterinary practices to cause hepatotoxic, nephrotoxic, neurotoxic, and reproductive development and endocrine system effects in humans and animals [29]. It can cause serious health hazards to non-target organisms including animal larvae, plants and humans, leading to death or hindering functional responses [30,31]. Therefore, it is of great significance to establish rapid and accurate detection of FL. Rao et al. [32] established a novel rapid pesticide extraction technology and successfully analyzed the FL content in farmland topsoil samples and egg samples in combination with UHPLLC-MS/MS. Liu et al. [33] synthesized europium-based metal-organic frameworks by hydrothermal method, and used low concentrations of FL to significantly quench the red fluorescence characteristics of europium-based metal-organic frameworks, and established a fluorescence sensor with a detectable concentration as low as 4.4 nmol/L FL. Wang et al. [34] developed a nano-plasma chip based on surface plasmon resonance (SPR), integrated the chip with a multi-channel spectral imaging system, and established a multi-channel nano-plasma platform for detecting FL. Nurdin et al. [35] prepared a molecularly imprinted polyaniline-modified graphene/TiO2 nanocomposite electrode, and constructed an FL chemical sensor with a detectable concentration of 0.74 mmol. As far as we know, there are no reports of the use of CDNFe@MIP that can be used as identification elements to identify FL, and as catalysts to catalyze sodium oxalate to reduce chloroauric acid signal amplification SERS/RRS dual-mode detection of FL. Therefore, in this paper, high catalytic active carbon dots doped with different elements were prepared, and their catalytic activity was compared. The best catalytic active carbon dots were selected as the nano-substrate to prepare CDNFe@MIP. Based on the catalytic and recognition bifunctional properties of CDNFe@MIP, a simple, rapid and sensitive SERS/RRS dual spectral analysis method for the detection of FL was established.

UPW-N15UV ultra-pure water machine (Shanghai Yidian Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China); KQ3200DB CNC ultrasonic cleaner (ultrasonic electric power 150 W, working frequency 40 KHz, Kunshan Ultrasonic Instrument Co., Ltd., Kunshan, China); JUPITER-B microwave disintegrator (2200 W, operating frequency 50 Hz, Shanghai Xin Yi Microwave Chemical Technology Co., Shanghai, China); DXR smart Raman spectrometer (The laser wavelength was 633 nm, the laser power was 5.0 mW, the slit was 50 μm, and the acquisition time was 8 s, Thermo, USA); Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High-Tech Company, Japan); TU-1901 double-beam UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Pu-analysis General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China); Fourier Transform infrared spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA); gas adsorption instrument (BSD-PS4 type, Japan); Nanoparticle size and Zeta potential analyzer (Malvern, UK); D/Max-3c X-ray powder diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan), and Thermofisher Talos F200s transmission electron microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) were used.

Sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4, SO); ferric chloride (Fecl3, 99 %); fipronil (FL, 97 %); α-methacrylic acid (α-MAA, 99 %); ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA, 98 %); 2,2′-azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN, 98 %); polyvinyl alcohol (95 %), all of the above products were purchased from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China; Ferrocene (Fe(C5H5)2, Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China); Urea (CH4N2O, Wuxi Prospect Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. China); trisodium citrate dihydrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. , China); chloroauric acid (HAuCl4, Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China); absolute alcohol (C2H5OH, Guangdong Guanghua Technology Co., Ltd., China); 10−5 mol/L Victoria Blue 4R (VB4r, Shanghai Shenggong Bioengineering Co., Ltd., China); Methanol (CH3OH, Xilong Science Co., Ltd., China); HCl (Xilong Science Co., Ltd., China); ethanol (C2H5OH, Xilong Science Co., Ltd. China); acetic acid (Xilong Science Co., Ltd., China). The reagents used were all analytical reagents, and the experimental water was ultrapure.

In a 100 mL conical flask, 109.2 mg of fipronil (0.25 mmol) and 0.170 mL of α-methacrylic acid (2 mmol) were added and dissolved with 30.0 mL of 1 % polyvinyl alcohol solution to form a pre-polymerized solution. After ultrasonication for 30 min, 1.89 mL ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (10 mmol) was added for ultrasonication for 20 min. Finally, 24.2 mg azodiisobutyronitrile was added, and the fipronil molecularly imprinted polymer was obtained by microwave digestion at 70 °C for 120 min. Then, the product was centrifuged at 12000 r/min for 10 min with 10 mL methanol-glacial acetic acid (volume ratio of 9:1) solution. The template molecules were eluted three times and dried at 45 °C for 12 h. According to the above steps without fipronil, NIP was prepared.

CD: In a 100 ml conical flask, 1 g of citric acid was added and dissolved with 30.0 ml of deionized water to form a clear solution, which was transferred to a PTFE autoclave. After sealing, the autoclave was heated at 180 °C for 5 h. The solution was cooled to room temperature with tap water, dialyzed with a 3500 Da dialysis bag for one night, and neutralized with NaOH solution to pH = 7.0 to obtain a C1 = 12.5 mg/mL CD solution.

CDN: In a 100 ml conical flask, 1 g of trisodium citrate and 0.8 g of urea were added and sonicated to form a clear solution by dissolution with 30.0 ml of deionized water, which was then placed in an ablution tank. After sealing, it was placed in a microwave digestion instrument. The temperature was set at 140 °C, the pressure was 5.0 atm, and the holding time was 10 min. After the reaction was completed, it was cooled to room temperature to obtain a light yellow clear solution. The total amount of carbon was added to calculate the C2 = 14.6 mg/ml CDN solution.

CDNFe: In a 100 ml conical flask, 1 g of trisodium citrate and 0.8 g of urea were added, followed by 0.01 g, 0.018 g, 0.025 g, and 0.8 g of ferrocene, respectively, which were dissolved in 30.0 ml of deionized water and ultrasonicated to form a clarified solution. Pour the solution into the microwave digestion jar and seal it, then put it into the microwave digestion instrument. The temperature was set at 140 °C, the pressure was 5.0 atm, and the holding time was 10 min. After the reaction was completed, it was cooled to room temperature to obtain a light yellow clear solution. Calculated by the amount of carbon added, the C3 = 14.8 mg/ml CD0.01NFe, C4 = 15 mg/ml CD0.018NFe, C5 = 15.2 mg/ml CD0.025NFe and C6 = 31.8 mg/ml CD0.8NFe solutions were obtained.

CDNFeCl: In a 100 ml conical flask, 1 g of trisodium citrate and 0.8 g of urea were added, followed by 0.01 g, 0.018 g, 0.025 g, and 0.8 g of ferric chloride, respectively, which were dissolved in 30.0 ml of deionized water and ultrasonicated to form a clarified solution. Pour the solution into the microwave digestion jar and seal it, then put it into the microwave digestion instrument. The temperature was set at 140 °C, the pressure was 5.0 atm, and the holding time was 10 min. After the reaction was completed, it was cooled to room temperature to obtain a light yellow clear solution. The CD0.01NFeCl, CD0.018NFeCl, CD0.025NFeCl, CD0.8NFeCl solutions with C7 C8 = C9 = C10 = 14.6 mg/ml were obtained by adding total carbon.

In a 100 ml conical flask, 109.2 mg of fipronil (0.25 mmol), 0.170 ml of α-methacrylic acid (2 mmol), and 5 ml of 13 mg/ml CDNFe were added and dissolved with 30.0 ml of 1 % polyvinyl alcohol solution to form a pre-polymerized solution. After ultrasonication for 30 min, 1.89 mL ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (10 mmol) was added for ultrasonication for 20 min. Finally, 24.2 mg azodiisobutyronitrile was added, and the fipronil molecularly imprinted polymer was obtained by microwave digestion at 70 °C for 120 min. Then, the product was centrifuged for 10 min with 10 mL methanol-glacial acetic acid (volume ratio 9∶1) solution at 12000 r/min, and the template molecule was eluted three times, and dried at 45 °C for 12 h, recorded as CDNFe@MIP. Follow the above steps without fipronil to prepare CDNF@NIP.

Referring to the above steps, CD@MIP was prepared with 12.5 mg/ml CD as nano-substrate, 14.6 mg/ml CDN as nano-substrate to prepare CDN@MIP, and 14.6 mg/ml CD0.018NFeCl as nano-substrate to prepare CDNFeCl@MIP.

In a 5 mL tube, 170 μL of 10.0 mg/L CDNFe@MIP and various concentrations of FL were added and allowed to stand for 20 min at 50 °C in a water bath. Then 75.0 μL of 5 mmol/L Na2C2O4, 100.0 μL of 10.0 mmol/L HCl, and 100.0 μL of 1.0 mg/L HAuCl4 were added, diluted to 2.0 mL with water, and heated at 70 °C for 16 min, and then removed and cooled with ice water. The RRS peaks of 380 nm (I380nm) and blank (I380nm)0 without FL were obtained by synchronous scanning with a fluorescence spectrometer, and ΔI380nm = I380nm-(I380nm)0 was calculated. Add 70 μl VB4r to the above system, and use Raman spectroscopy to obtain the signal value at 1615 cm−1 (I1615cm-1) and blank (I1615cm-1)0 without FL, and calculate ΔI1615cm-1 = I1615cm-1-(I1615cm-1)0.

Highly fluorescent CDNFe was prepared by simple microwave method. Using CDNFe as the nano-substrate, FL and MAA were mixed to form a prepolymer after sonication. Then, by microwave irradiation, nanoprobe CDNFe@MIP with catalytic activity and specific FL recognition were synthesized. Due to the reducibility of SO, HAuCl4 can be reduced to AuNPs as nanoindicator and SERS substrate, so a HAuCl4–SO nano indicator was established. CDs have good catalytic effect, and doped carbon dots with different elements have better catalytic effect on the HAuCl4–SO indicator nanoreaction, which can generate more AuNPs. AuNPs had a strong RRS peak at 380 nm, and had a strong SERS signal peak at 1615 cm−1 after adding VB4r molecular probes. When CDNFe was encapsulated by MIP, the core-shell structure CDNFe@MIP nanoprobe was formed, and the particle size increases. However, in the presence of FL, the template molecule-specific recognition site on the surface of the CDNFe@MIP would be occupied by FL to form a CDNFe@MIP-FL complex, which hindered the contact between internal CDNFe and external indicator reaction system. Thus, the catalytic activity of CDNFe@MIP nanozyme was inhibited, and the formation of AuNPs was reduced, resulting in a decrease in the SERS/RRS signal of the system. Based on this, a dual-mode sensing analysis platform for SERS/RRS for FL detection with high sensitivity and selectivity can be established (Fig. 1 ).

The Flu/Abs/RRS spectra of the nanomaterial CDNFe/MIP/CDNFe@MIP were shown in Fig. S1. At an excitation wavelength of 320 nm, CDNFe had a fluorescence peak at 450 nm, and its peak gradually increased with its concentration (Fig. S1A). The Abs peak of CDNFe appeared at 350 nm, and its absorbance gradually increased with the increase of CDNFe concentration (Fig. S1B). The RRS spectrum of CDNFe showed an absorption peak at 350 nm and weakened, and the fluorescence peak at 450 nm gradually increased (Fig. S1C). Fluorescence properties associated with CDNFe@MIP excitation were analyzed by fluorescence spectrophotometer study (Fig. S1D). Taking 1 mg/mL CDNFe@MIP as an example, the corresponding emission peaks were recorded at excitation wavelengths of 240, 260, 280, 300, 320, 340, 360, and 380 nm at a voltage of 350 V, and the results showed that there were no fluorescence peaks CDNFe@MIP. The UV spectrum scan of MIP and CDNFe@MIP showed that they did not appear obvious Abs peak, and their absorbance gradually increased with the increase of MIP and CDNFe@MIP concentration (Figs. S1E and F). Both MIP and CDNFe@MIP had an RRS peak at 380 nm, and the peak increased gradually with the increase of MIP and CDNFe@MIP concentrations (Figs. S1G and H).

Surface chemical group identification and elemental analysis of CDN/CDNFe/MIP/CDNFe@MIP were performed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Fig. S1I). In FT-IR spectroscopy, for CDN and CDNFe, the wide absorption band at 3348 cm−1 was related to O–H and N–H tensile vibrations, the absorption band at 2360 cm−1 was assigned to O=C=O, 1398 cm−1 was derived from C–N flexural vibration, 1608 cm−1 was C=N and C=O tensile vibrations, and the wide absorption band at 3348 cm−1 was related to O–H and N–H tensile vibrations [36]. In addition, CDNFe had a faint Fe–O peak at 520 cm−1 [37]. For MIP/CDNFe@MIP, the peak at 2958 cm−1 was derived from the tensile vibration of C–H, and the peak at 1728 cm−1 was caused by the tensile vibration of C=O in MAA and EGDMA, and the characteristic peaks at 1456 cm−1 and 1389 cm−1 were caused by C–H bending vibrations at –CH3 and –CH2, and the bending vibrations of C–O triggered the characteristic peaks at 1161 cm−1.

In addition, XRD profiles of prepared materials were reported in the range of 2θ = 5–70° (Fig. S1J). For CDNFe, there was an amorphous signal near approximately 23.8°, showing a typical disordered graphite structure, with peaks observed at 28.4° attributed to the Fe–N phase [38]. MIP, CDNFe@MIP and CDNFe@NIP all had diffraction peaks around 21°, suggesting that they were similar in crystallographic structure and all had amorphous properties [39].

XPS spectra further revealed the elemental composition of MIP and CDNFe@MIP. Two main peaks, 285.0 e V and 532.0 e V, were observed in their measurement spectra, which were attributed to C and O elements (Fig. 2 F). High-resolution spectroscopic verification of C1s (Fig. 2G) showed the presence of C–C/C=C (284.8 eV), C–O (286.3 eV), and C=O (288.3 eV). In the high-resolution spectrum of O1S (Fig. 2H), two peaks were fitted at 532.1 eV and 533.4 eV, attributed to C–O and C=O, respectively.

The prepared CDNFe, CDNFe@MIP and MIPs were scanned by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and their morphologies were recorded. From Fig. 2A, many dispersed particles with sizes in the range of 1–7 nm could be observed, and they had a bright fluorescence field under ultraviolet light irradiation. From Fig. 2B and C, it could be seen that the CDNFe@MIP and MIP particles were spherical and wrapped in a thin translucent polymer layer with uneven imprint sites on the surface and a particle size of about 1.5 μm. From Fig. 2D, it could be seen that the particle morphology of the AuNPs generated in the system was irregular, and the average size was 50 nm. In addition, from the corresponding energy spectrum, we can see the five characteristic peaks of the Au element (Fig. 2E), which can prove the generation of AuNPs in the system.

The particle size and zeta potential of the related materials were scanned, and the results were as follows (Fig. S2). The CDNFe potential is −8.23 mV (Fig. S2A), the MIP potential is −7.56 mV (Fig. S2B), the CDNFe@MIP potential is −7.41 mV (Fig. S2C), and the CDNFe@NIP potential is −8.11 mV (Fig. S2D). It could be seen that the prepared materials have good stability. MIP and CDNFe@MIP were scanned for particle size (Fig. S2E), and it was found that the particle size of MIP was 400 nm, and the particle size of CDNFe@MIP was 530 nm, indicating that the particle size of MIP was increased after encapsulating CDNFe.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and BJH pore size distribution of MIP, CDNFe@MIP and CDNFe@MIPFL were determined by BSD-PS4 gas adsorption instrument as shown in Fig. 3 . According to the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) formula, the specific surface areas of MIP, CDNFe@MIP and CDNFe@MIPFL were 0.40 m2g-1, 2.06 m2g-1 and 0.039 m2g-1, respectively. It could be seen that MIP encapsulated CDNFe to form a core-shell structure CDNFe@MIP nanoparticle with big surface, and the water-eluted CDNFe@MIPFL had a very small specific surface area. The pore size distribution was illustrated in Fig. 3B, where the average pore sizes of MIP, CDNFe@MIP and CDNFe@MIPFL were 25.0 nm, 29.0 nm and 6.6 nm, respectively.

The prepared CDNFe was placed in a refrigerator at 4 °C for 2 weeks, and the FL, RRS and Abs signal changes of the three at different times were scanned and recorded (Figs. S3A and B). It could be seen that the signal strength does not change much and has good stability. In addition, we also investigated the stability of CDNFe in different concentrations of NaCl solution. The results show that the RRS, FL and Abs signals of CDNFe tend to be stable in different concentrations of NaCl solution (Figs. S3C and D). The RRS signals of MIP and CDNFe@MIP were smaller at different times (Fig. S3E), and the RRS signals tended to be stable in NaCl solution at different concentrations (Fig. S3F). The results showed that the prepared CDNFe/MIP/CDNFe@MIP had good stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RRS spectra of catalytic and analytical systems

ce:section.ce:para: It was found that by controlling the amounts of HAuCl4, Na2O2C4 and HCl, a redox system producing trace amounts of AuNPs could be established. After the addition of carbon dots or MIPs in the system, Na2O2C4 reduced HAuCl4 to form a large number of AuNPs, and AuNPs produced two resonance Rayleigh scattering peaks at 380 nm and 550 nm, respectively. From Fig. 4 , it could be seen that the resonance Rayleigh scattering peak at 380 nm was larger and more sensitive, so the 380 nm wavelength was chosen as a follow-up study. We explored the catalytic effect of different doped carbon dots CD/CDN/CD0.01NFe/CD0.018NFe/CD0.025NFe/CD0.8NFe/CD0.01NFecl/CD0.018NFecl /CD0.025NFecl/CD0.8NFecl on the system through experiments. The results showed that the RRS signal increased linearly with the concentration of carbon dots in a certain concentration range, and the catalytic slope of CD0.018NFe was the largest (Table S1). According to the slope method, the catalytic effect is the best. Therefore, we chose CD0.018NFe as the core to prepare CDNFe@MIP for further research. CDNFe@MIP was a nanosurface molecularly imprinted with CD0.018NFe as the core to form a core-shell structure, and the molecularly imprinted sites were located on the surface of the nanomaterials. After FL elution, part of the CD0.018NFe was exposed, so it had strong catalytic ability (Fig. S5D). At the same time, the catalytic effect of MIP/CD@MIP/CDN@MIP/CDNFecl@MIP/NIP/ MIPFL/CDNFe@NIP/CDNFe@MIPFL on the system was also investigated (Fig. S5). It was found that the catalytic slope of CDNFe@MIP in the molecularly imprinted polymer KCDNFe@MIP = 827.69 was the highest, so its catalytic effect was the best (Fig. S5).

Further research found that in the CDNFe@MIP-HAuCl4-Na2C2O4–HCl catalytic system, after FL was added, FL was adsorbed on the molecularly imprinted sites on the surface of the CDNFe@MIP nanoprobe to form CDNFe@MIP-FL complex. Further study found that in the CDNFe@MIP-HAuCl4-Na2C2O4–HCl catalytic system, after FL was added, FL was adsorbed on the molecularly imprinted sites on the surface of the CDNFe@MIP nanoprobe to form a CDNFe@MIP-FL complex, which hindered the contact between the internal CDNFe and the external indicator reaction system, inhibited the catalytic activity of the CDNFe@MIP nanozyme, and reduced the generated AuNPs, resulting in a decrease in the RRS signal of the system (Fig. 4D). In a certain concentration range, with the increase of FL concentration, the RRS signal showed a linear decreasing trend. Based on this, a new RRS sensing analysis platform for detecting FL was established (Fig. 4D). NIP and CDNFe@NIP only showed weak catalytic activity due to the absence of molecular imprinting sites and smoother molecular surface (Figs. S5H and I). MIPFL and CDNFe@MIPFL were filled with water during the elution process, and most of FL was filled on the surface of CDNFe@MIP, which weakened its catalytic effect. It also verified that when FL was combined with CDNFe@MIP, its catalytic ability was reduced (Figs. S5F and G). At the same time, the specific recognition effect of MIP/CD@MIP/CDN@MIP/CDNFecl@MIP on FL was also investigated, and it could be used to determine FL with RRS (Fig. 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS spectra of catalytic and analytical systems

ce:section.ce:para: Under the experimental conditions, the HAuCl4–Na2O2C4 system can produce trace amounts of AuNPs. When carbon dots or MIP catalysts were added to the system, HAuCl4 was reduced to generate a large number of indicator products AuNPs. After the addition of VB4r molecular probe, there was a strong SERS signal at 1615 cm−1, and its SERS intensity increased linearly with the amount of carbon dots and MIP. According to the slope method, we also explored the catalytic effect of different doped carbon dots CD/CDN/CD0.01NFe/CD0.018NFe/CD0.025NFe/ CD0.8NFe/CD0.01NFecl/CD0.018NFecl/CD0.025NFecl/CD0.8NFecl on the system. In a certain concentration range, the SERS signal increases linearly with the concentration of carbon dots (Fig. S6), and CD0.018NFe has the largest catalytic slope (Fig. S6D), so the catalytic effect is the strongest, which is consistent with the RRS catalytic system. At the same time, the reaction effect of MIP/CD@MIP/CDN@MIP/CDNFe@MIP/CDNFecl@MIP/NIP/ MIPFL/CDNFe@NIP/CDNFe@MIPFL as catalyst on the system was also investigated (Fig. S7). It was found that CDNFe@MIP had the highest catalytic slope, so the catalytic effect was the best, and the results were also consistent with the RRS catalytic system.

Consistent with the RRS system. Under the experimental conditions, after the addition of FL, the catalytic effect of MIP/CD@MIP/CDN@MIP/CDNFe@MIP/CDNFecl@MIP decreased, the generated AuNPs decreased, and the SERS signal decreased (Fig. 5 ). In a certain range, with the increase of FL concentration, the decrease of SERS signal is linearly correlated. Based on this, a new SERS sensing analysis platform for FL detection could be established. It can be seen from Table S2 that CDNFe@MIP has a wider detection range and lower detection limit for FL. Therefore, the CDNFe@MIP-HAuCl4-Na2C2O4–HCl analysis system was selected to detect FL, and the sensitivity was higher than that of RRS.

Na2C2O4 has a certain reducibility, which can reduce Au3+ to Au0, and its C2O4 2− is oxidized to CO2. Therefore, in this experiment, a redox system that can generate AuNPs was established by controlling the amount of HAuCl4 and Na2C2O4. In a certain range of catalyst concentration, the AuNPs generated in the system were synchronously scanned by a fluorescence spectrophotometer to obtain RRS signals. After adding VB4r, the SERS signal was obtained by Raman spectroscopy, and the RRS signal and SERS signal were linearly related to the catalyst concentration. In a certain concentration range, the catalytic activity of carbon dots on HAuCl4– Na2C2O4 system showed a linear relationship (Table S1). Because of the small particle size and special functional groups of carbon dots, they showed good catalytic ability. According to the slope method, we know that the greater the slope, the higher the catalytic activity. The CDNFe doped with N and Fe elements has higher catalytic activity than other carbon dots. This is because the doping of N and Fe elements into the carbon dots makes CDNFe have more electrons and special functional groups Fe–N equal, which promotes the electron transfer in the system. MIP could specifically recognize FL, but its catalytic activity was weak, and the catalytic slope KSERS was only 442.25. The catalytic activity of CDNFe@MIP prepared by using CDNFe as the nano-substrate was significantly improved. The catalytic slope KSERS was 3408.5, and the catalytic ability was increased by 7.7 times.

In order to understand the catalytic mechanism of CDNFe@MIP, we investigated the catalytic ability of different molecularly imprinted polymers CDNFe@MIP, CDNFe@NIP, CDNFe@MIPFL, MIP, NIP, MIPFL. From Table S2, it could be seen that the slope KCDNFe@MIP > KMIP > KCDNFe@NIP > KCDNFe@MIPFL > KNIP > KMIPFL. According to the slope method, we know that the greater the slope, the higher the catalytic activity. The CDNFe@MIP formed by encapsulating CDNFe with MIP. After the template molecule was eluted, some CDNFe was exposed to the system to promote the catalysis of the system, so the catalytic activity of CDNFe@MIP was stronger than that of MIP. The MIPFL and CDNFe@MIPFL of FL eluted with water were filled in the imprinting sites because most of the FL was not eluted, so that the internal CDNFe could not contact with the reaction system and the surface of the imprinted polymer was smoother, resulting in a decrease in catalytic activity, while CDNFe@MIPFL had a small amount of CDNFe exposed, which made the catalytic activity higher than that of MIPFL. After FL and CDNFe@MIP were stationary for a certain period of time, it was found that FL showed a linear decrease in I380nm and I1617cm-1 signal intensity within 5–500 nmol/L (Figs. 4D and 5D). Due to the binding of FL to the specific recognition site on the surface of CDNFe@MIP, the contact between the internal CDNFe and the external reaction system was hindered, resulting in a decrease in its catalytic ability and a decrease in the amount of AuNPs indicating the reaction. It can be seen that the CDNFe@MIP nanoprobe can be used as a catalyst and a bifunctional nanozyme as a recognition element. After MIP encapsulated CDNFe and then eluted the template molecule, more imprinted nanoporouses formed and part of the CDNFe was exposed to promote the catalysis of the system, so the catalytic activity of the CDNFe@MIP was stronger than that of CDNFe@MIPFL. The CDNFe@MIPFL were eluted with water, because the FL molecules were not eluted and filled in the imprint site, so that the internal CDNFe cannot contact the redox nanoreaction and its reactants, and the surface was smoother, resulting in reduced catalytic activity. Moreover, according to the size of the specific surface, it could be seen that the catalytic activity of CDNFe@MIP was stronger, because CDNFe@MIP had a larger specific surface, the specific surface of CDNFe@MIP was 2.06 m2g-1, while the specific surface of CDNFe@MIPFL was only 0.039 m2g-1. Solvents play an important role in the synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymers. Prepare CDNFe@MIP with 30 mL of 1 % polyvinyl alcohol/acetonitrile/methanol solution, respectively. The specific experimental steps refer to 2.6, and it can be seen from the preparation of the sample that methanol and acetonitrile are transparent solutions and have no product; Polyvinyl alcohol as solvent is a powder with a higher yield. Therefore, polyvinyl alcohol was selected as the solvent.

FL-CDNFe@MIP-HAuCl4-Na2C2O4–HCl was used and the ΔI380nm as an index to investigate the influence of the preparation conditions of CDNFe@MIP on the determination of FL. In order to select the most suitable CDNFe@MIP preparation conditions, we used univariate analysis to optimize the preparation conditions of the CDNFe@MIP, namely functional monomer, crosslinker, preparation temperature and time, and took the ΔI380nm maximum as the selection basis. For specific experimental steps, refer to 2.6. The results showed that (Fig. S8), when FL was 0.25 mmol, MAA was 2 mmol, EGDMA was 10 mmol, the molar ratio of the three was 1:8:40, the initiator was 1 % of the mass of the monomer and the crosslinking agent, and the CDNFe@MIP prepared at 70 °C for 2 h had good sensitivity to the determination of FL.

According to the experimental method, the conditions of FL-CDNFe@MIP-HAuCl4-Na2C2O4–HCl analysis system were optimized. When the experimental conditions were 0.05 g/L HAuCl4 (Fig. S9A), 0.1875 mmol/L Na2C2O4 (Fig. S9B), 0.5 mmol/L HCl (Fig. S9C), 0.85 mg/L CDNFe@MIP (Fig. S9D), standing at 50 °C for 20 min (Figs. S9G and H), and reacting at 70 °C for 16 min (Figs. S9E and F), the ΔI380 of the analysis system was the largest. Therefore, these conditions were selected as the experimental conditions.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, the concentration of FL has a good linear relationship with the SERS/RRS signal value. The regression equation of the SERS system is ΔI1617cm-1 = 5.8712CFL-10.676, the linear correlation coefficient is 0.9912, and the linear range is 5.0–500 ng/L. The regression equation of the RRS system is ΔI380nm = 1.7688CFL+17.355, the linear correlation coefficient is 0.984, and the linear range is 5.0–500 ng/L. The standard deviation Sd was obtained by measuring the blank sample 11 times in parallel, and the detection limit and the quantitative limit of the method were calculated according to the formula LOD = 3 × Sd/N (N is the slope of the working curve) and LOQ = 10 × Sd/N. At the same time, the specific recognition comparison of surface molecularly imprinted polymers prepared by nucleation of different matrices was compared, and it was found that the SERS detection limit of CDNFe@MIP was lowest (Table 1 ). Compared with the reported methods for detecting FL (Table S3), it could be seen that the method has very high sensitivity [32–34,40,41].

The effect of coexisting substances (structural analogues, anions and cations) on RRS detection of 100 ng/L FL was studied by method. The experimental results showed that the relative error was within ±10 %, 10 times of dinotefuran, acetamiprid, Fe3+, Ca2+, F−, I−, 50 times of isocarbophos, glyphosate, Fe2+, Mn2+, and 100 times of thiamethoxam, Co2+, Mn2+, SO4 2−, Na+, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, Br−, K+, Ba2+, IO3 2− did not interfere with the determination of FL (Fig. S10). In order to consider the matrix effect of the actual sample, the actual sample detected with FL was diluted and measured with the CDNFe@MIP-HAuCl4-Na2C2O4–HCl system as a blank. The RRS detection limit of the actual sample was calculated to be 3.3 ng/L, and the limit of quantitation was 11.0 ng/L. Compared with the detection limit of the standard, the relative error is within 10 %. Therefore, the method has good selectivity and can be applied to actual samples.

Egg samples were purchased from different local fresh supermarkets. The sample was processed according to the reference [42]. Break an egg into a 500 mL beaker and stir it with an egg beater until well-distributed. From the beaker, 5 g of homogenized egg samples were weighed in a 50 mL polypropylene centrifuge tube, and 10 mL of acetonitrile was added. The mixture in the centrifuge tube was vortexed and mixed for 1 min, and then sonicated for 10 min. Then 1 g NaCl and 3 g anhydrous MgSO4 were added to the centrifuge tube, immediately shaken for 1 min, and then centrifuged at - 4 °C and 10000 rpm for 5 min. After that, the centrifuge tube was placed in a refrigerator at −18 °C for 20 min to separate the lipid from the solvent. The supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and transferred to a volumetric flask, and diluted with water to 10 mL, which was used as a backup sample detection solution. According to the experimental method, the FL-CDNFe@MIP-HAuCl4-Na2C2O4–HCl system was selected to detect FL in the actual sample for 5 times. The average value of the 5 measured values was taken as the result, and the sample without addition was used as the blank control. A certain FL content was added to the sample and determined according to the experimental method, then the recovery was calculated. The results showed that the RSD of the system for eggs was between 3.3 and 8.1 %, and the recovery rate was between 94.6 and 104.5 % (Table S4). According to the maximum residue limit (MRL) of the national food safety standard maximum residue limit of pesticides in food (GB 2763-2021), the MRL of fipronil on eggs was 0.02 mg/kg, and the FL content in the measured samples was much lower than the national standard. To investigate the validity of the method, the samples were determined by gas chromatography (GC) method. The results were consistent with the GC method.

In this paper, fipronil was used as a template molecule to prepare a core-shell structure doped carbon dot surface molecularly imprinted polymethacrylic acid nanoprobe (CDNFe@MIP) with high catalytic amplification and specific recognition function. They were characterized by TEM, SEM, XPS and FTIR techniques. The catalytic ability of CDNFe coated by MIP decreased, but the catalytic ability of CDNFe coated by MIP was significantly improved, which could better amplify the Na2C2O4–HAuCl4 nano-indicating reaction. By heating, FL was promoted to enter the imprinting site on the surface of CDNFe@MIP, which reduced its catalytic activity and generated fewer AuNPs. In a certain concentration range of FL, the SERS/RRS signal decreased linearly with the increase of FL concentration. Based on this, a SERS/RRS dual-mode nanosurface molecularly imprinted polymer sensing platform for simple, high sensitivity and selectivity detection of FL was established. The analysis method was applied to the actual sample, and the RSD was 3.3–8.1 %, and the recovery rate was between 94.6 and 104.5 %.
